title: Best VPN for Streaming in 2026: How to Access Netflix, HBO, Disney+ from Anywhere
description: Streaming services geo-block content by territory. Here is which VPNs actually work for Netflix, BBC iPlayer, and Disney+ in 2026, and what to look for before subscribing.
content:
## Why Streaming Services Block You Based on Location

When you try to watch a show on Netflix while traveling abroad and see "not available in your region," that is geo-blocking in action. It is not a technical glitch. It is deliberate.

Streaming services do not own the content they show. They license it, territory by territory. A studio might sell the streaming rights to Netflix for the US, to Amazon for Germany, and to a local broadcaster for France. Each deal is separate, and each deal includes a contractual obligation to block viewers outside that territory. Netflix US and Netflix UK are different catalogs because the licensing agreements are different, not because Netflix wants to be inconvenient.

This creates a situation where the same person, watching the same service, sees a different library depending on which country their internet connection appears to come from. A VPN routes your traffic through a server in another country, so the streaming service sees that server's location rather than your actual one. You appear to be in the US, and you get the US catalog.

## The Cat-and-Mouse Problem

Netflix, Disney+, BBC iPlayer, and most major streaming services actively work to block VPN IP addresses. When they detect traffic coming from a known VPN server, they block that IP address. The streaming service and the VPN provider are effectively in a permanent arms race.

VPN providers respond by rotating to new IP addresses constantly. But this creates three practical realities for subscribers:

**Not every VPN works for every service.** A VPN with 5,000 servers does not necessarily unblock Netflix. What matters is whether the provider actively maintains and rotates the specific servers used for streaming, and whether Netflix has caught up to their current batch.

**What works today may not work next month.** A VPN that unblocks Netflix US today might be blocked in six weeks when Netflix flags those IP addresses. Providers with large budgets and active server management recover faster. Providers that do not prioritize streaming may not recover at all.

**You need a track record, not just a claim.** A VPN's marketing page will always say it works for Netflix. What matters is whether independent users and reviewers consistently report it working over time, not whether the product page says so.

## VPNs That Consistently Work for Streaming in 2026

**ExpressVPN ($8.32/month, annual plan)**

ExpressVPN is the most consistently reliable option for Netflix across multiple regions. The company invests heavily in streaming-specific server maintenance and tends to recover quickly when Netflix blocks a batch of their IPs. Their MediaStreamer feature (a DNS-based tool that works on devices that cannot run the VPN app directly, like smart TVs and game consoles) adds coverage beyond what the standard app provides.

At $8.32 per month on an annual plan, it is the most expensive option here. The premium is essentially a subscription to consistent, actively-maintained streaming access. For someone who uses a VPN primarily to access streaming content, the reliability justifies the cost.

**NordVPN ($3.99/month, annual plan)**

NordVPN includes SmartPlay, a technology built into every NordVPN server that combines VPN with Smart DNS functionality to optimize for streaming. In practice this means you do not have to find and test specific servers for Netflix, the way you do with some cheaper VPNs. SmartPlay handles server selection automatically.

NordVPN works reliably for Netflix US, Netflix UK, and Netflix Japan. It has had occasional issues with Netflix blocking specific server clusters, but the company resolves these within days in most cases. At $3.99 per month, it is the best value for streaming access among major providers.

**Surfshark ($2.49/month, annual plan)**

Surfshark is the cheapest option that still delivers reliable streaming access. Unlike most VPNs, Surfshark allows unlimited simultaneous device connections on a single subscription. For households where multiple people stream on different devices, this is a meaningful advantage.

The streaming performance is slightly behind ExpressVPN and NordVPN in terms of consistency, but Surfshark invests in server maintenance for the major streaming services. It works for Netflix US and UK reliably, and for Disney+ with no significant issues. At $2.49 per month, the gap between Surfshark and the more expensive providers is hard to justify for most users.

**Private Internet Access ($2.03/month, annual plan)**

PIA runs one of the largest VPN server networks available, with servers in more countries and cities than most competitors. This makes it useful for accessing niche streaming services or content libraries in less common markets. For mainstream services like Netflix US and UK, it works but requires some server selection effort rather than the automatic optimization of NordVPN's SmartPlay.

**Mullvad (Not Recommended for Streaming)**

Mullvad is the best VPN available for privacy. It accepts anonymous payment, stores no identifiable user data, and has been independently audited. But it is not built for streaming. Mullvad does not maintain streaming-specific servers, and many services block Mullvad exit nodes. If privacy is your primary goal and streaming is secondary, Mullvad is an excellent choice. If streaming is the main reason you want a VPN, it is the wrong tool.

## Netflix Catalogs Worth Accessing

Not all Netflix libraries are equal. Here are the ones most worth accessing through a VPN.

**Netflix US** is the largest catalog, with over 5,000 titles compared to roughly 3,000 in the UK library. The US library includes a broader selection of Hollywood content and has the highest concentration of original series available on day one.

**Netflix Japan** has the strongest anime catalog of any regional library, plus a large selection of Studio Ghibli films and Asian-exclusive drama series. For anime viewers in particular, the Japan library is noticeably better than what is available in European or North American markets.

**Netflix UK** carries strong BBC co-productions and European drama series that are not available on the US platform. UK Netflix also gets certain licensed content at different times than the US library.

**Netflix Canada** has a reasonable selection of Canadian content and, at any given time, some US series that are temporarily available in Canada but not the US due to licensing timing.

## BBC iPlayer: UK Only, But Free

BBC iPlayer is the most aggressively geo-blocked major streaming service. It requires both a UK IP address and a free account creation. The account creation does not require a UK address, but it does require a UK IP at the time you register.

Once you have an account and a UK VPN connection, the content is genuinely free. The full BBC archive, live TV channels, and new releases are all included. For British drama, documentaries, and news, iPlayer is one of the best streaming libraries available.

ExpressVPN and NordVPN's UK servers are the most reliable for iPlayer. BBC is active at blocking VPN traffic, so server performance on this service specifically is worth checking before committing to a provider.

## Disney+: Easier Than Netflix

Disney+ is less aggressive about VPN blocking than Netflix. Most premium VPNs will unblock Disney+ without significant issues. The main regional difference in the Disney+ catalog is the Hotstar library: in India, Disney+ carries a large Hotstar catalog (Indian films, cricket, Bollywood) that is not available in Western markets. Accessing the Indian Disney+ library via VPN works with most major providers but requires an Indian billing method for the subscription itself, which limits practical access.

For standard Disney+ content in the US versus Europe, the differences are smaller than on Netflix. Most Marvel and Star Wars content releases at the same time globally.

## Speed Requirements for Streaming

A VPN adds some overhead because your traffic is encrypted and routed through an additional server. How much speed you lose depends on the VPN provider, the server distance, and your base connection speed.

Streaming requires:
- 1080p HD: 5 Mbps minimum
- 4K HDR: 25 Mbps minimum

A good VPN should retain at least 70 percent of your base connection speed. If your home internet runs at 100 Mbps, you should still have 70 Mbps or more through the VPN. That leaves substantial headroom above the streaming requirements, even for 4K.

Where speed becomes a concern is on very long-distance connections. Connecting to a Japan server from Europe introduces significant latency and can drop speeds below 4K requirements on slower base connections. For accessing Japan Netflix specifically, a base speed of at least 50 Mbps is advisable to maintain buffer-free 4K.

ExpressVPN and NordVPN consistently outperform other providers on speed retention in independent tests. Surfshark is close behind. This is one of the reasons the premium providers justify their higher price for streaming use.

## Choosing the Right VPN for Streaming

If Netflix US is your primary target, NordVPN at $3.99 per month is the best value. SmartPlay handles server selection automatically, the speed is good, and the price is competitive.

If you need consistent access to multiple services across multiple regions, BBC iPlayer included, ExpressVPN is worth the higher cost. The reliability across services is consistently better than competitors, and the MediaStreamer feature covers devices the app cannot run on.

If you are on a tight budget and primarily need Netflix and Disney+, Surfshark at $2.49 per month covers the core use case at the lowest price of any reliable option.

The one thing to avoid is subscribing to a free VPN for streaming. Free VPNs run small server networks that streaming services block quickly and permanently, because free providers cannot afford the constant server rotation that paid providers invest in. Free VPN plus Netflix almost never works, and the connection will be slow even when it does.
